data.title: PotlatchDeltic Positioned To Have Earnings Bump When Lumber Prices Normalize
data.description:
Timber REITs contain multiple businesses across the forest industry vertical, ranging from land ownership to the sale of specialized wood products. Rayonier (RYN) is mostly on the land side of the business as it does not own its mills, while Weyerhaeuser (WY) is the big conglomerate with full exposure to each section of the industry. PotlatchDeltic Corporation (NASDAQ:PCH) aka “Potlatch” sits primarily on the lumber and wood products side. While PCH does own its land and derives profits from various land aspects, its EBITDA is far more lumber related than that of its peers.

In 2024, this lumber tilt was unfavorable, as lumber prices were at rock bottom levels. Indeed, PCH underperformed its less lumber-focused peers.

Going forward, however, increased lumber exposure could work in its favor.

The Buy Thesis

Potlatch is trading at deeply discounted levels (73% of net asset value) due to recent weakness of commodity pricing, but the fundamental outlook for lumber has turned favorable. Various factors point to a strong recovery in margins for PCH’s mills, which sets up for EBITDA to nearly double from 2024 levels. With PCH stock priced for its trough EBITDA, the fundamental upswing that we see coming is not yet priced in.

This article will discuss various supply and demand factors which collectively point to higher lumber prices ahead. We then explore the impact it would have on PCH earnings, as well as implications for the company’s valuation.

Supply of lumber declining

The Wall Street Journal reports:

“Canfor will close two more sawmills in British Columbia and choke back output at pine mills in South Carolina and Georgia due to languishing prices for two-by-fours and rising U.S. tariffs on Canadian lumber exports.”

Interfor Corp. is also reducing mill operations, as reported again by the WSJ.

“One of North America's largest lumber producers said it would reduce output by at least 15% for the rest of 2024, increasing curtailments it made earlier this year amid tumbling demand and prices for two-by-fours. Interfor, which owns sawmills across Canada, the U.S. South and in the Pacific Northwest, said it would reduce output by as much as 350 million board through December.”

These closures are largely par for the course, with financial strain being felt ubiquitously by mill operators. Trading Economics believes the combination of aforementioned closures along with curtailments from Western Forest Products will reduce North American lumber capacity by 3 billion board feet:

“Reducing North American lumber capacity by over 3 billion board feet in 2024.”

These closures and curtailments seem to mostly be driven by lumber prices being so far below equilibrium that margins are break even or negative for much of the industry.

Timber REIT cost advantage will lead to market share gains

The timber REITs have certain cost advantages that come with their scale as well as owning their timberlands which reduces cost of inputs. Rather than buying timber to feed their mills, they grow it. This has allowed the timber REITs to maintain strongly positive EBITDA in 2024 despite the challenges to the industry. Potlatch, for example, is on pace for $223.5 million EBITDA in 2024.

PCH’s wood products segment EBITDA looks similar to that of mill competitors, coming in at negative $9.5 million in 3Q24. However, the mills are buoyed by the much more stable timberlands segment, which had +35.8M EBITDA in the same period.

Thus, the vertical integration allows timber REITs to remain profitable during a period of time when mills are broadly struggling and positions timber REITs generally and PCH specifically to grow market share as competitors shutter their mills.

As the supply of lumber wanes, demand seems to be showing signs of life.

Increased lumber demand

We believe demand for housing is about to inflect upward, as we discussed more extensively here.

Since that article was written, encouraging data has come out from the homebuilders. The November NAHB survey revealed significant gains in homebuilder confidence.

While the overall index increasing to 46 from 43 is positive, the big gains came from homebuilder outlook for next 6 months sales reaching 64.

Homebuilding activity might also increase due to reduced regulation and/or Federal lands being opened up for homebuilding. Diana Olick of CNBC discusses it here.

Impact on lumber pricing

Improved demand and reduced supply would typically cause prices to rise, and it has. Lumber futures rose over $600 for the first time in 2 years.

In addition to being a nearly 50% rise since the lows earlier this year, it is particularly notable because it is happening in November, a month of seasonally low demand.

Homebuilding activity slows down substantially in the winter because construction risks snow interference. Remodeling activity also slows, as people would rather not have missing walls when it’s cold outside.

With lower demand, lumber prices tend to be fairly low in November. I have marked each November of the past 5 years in yellow below.

Thus, this over $600 pricing happening during a seasonally weak month portends further price increases as spring homebuilding season approaches.

There are 2 key questions that I want to address here:

  1. What is the equilibrant price of lumber?
  2. How much do PCH’s earnings flex with lumber prices?

Equilibrant price of lumber ~$700

Inflation has been high for both labor and equipment, so the equilibrant price of lumber will be substantially higher than in previous cycles.

We can deduce from widespread mill closures that lumber futures prices in the $400-$550 range are not high enough for most operators.

Similarly, we know from the rampant mill openings and mill overclocking in 2021 and 2022 that lumber prices above $1000 are well above equilibrant pricing.

That gives the wide range of $550 to $1000. Based on industry commentary on various conference calls and normal industry level margins, I suspect the equilibrant price of futures is somewhere around $700.

Note that realizations for mills are regional or even hyperlocal, and that Random Lengths is a better proxy for realizations than lumber futures, but we are talking in broad brushstrokes here and futures are readily accessible and generally fairly close.

PCH EBITDA at various lumber prices

PCH’s EBITDA varies greatly with lumber prices.

Its 3Q24 10-Q reported negative $9.5 million EBITDDA as shown earlier, which is of course during low lumber pricing, but in 1Q22 the wood products segment delivered $150 million EBITDDA.

This variance has led to substantial volatility in PCH’s annual EBITDA numbers.

When lumber prices were extremely high in 2021 and 2022, PHC earned $652.8 million and $574.2 million EBITDA, respectively.

In 2023 and 2024 when lumber prices were very low, PCH earned $200.2 million and an estimated $223.5 million, respectively.

The stock market has a bad habit of extrapolating recent performance, so PCH stock soared in 2021/2022 and plummeted in 2023/2024.

Given the cyclicality of the industry, we prefer to average over the cycle. Over the past 6 years, PCH has averaged $368.65 million EBITDA annually. Given the decline in PCH’s price, that is against an enterprise value of $4.196B.

If we consider this the normalized average level of earnings, PCH is trading at a trailing EV/EBITDA of 11.38X. That is a trailing 6-year EBITDA yield of 8.78% which is quite cheap relative to the rest of the market. I think it represents substantial undervaluation, which is further reflected in the steep discount to NAV at which PCH trades.

Given reduced supply and higher demand for lumber along with PCH’s market share gains from large competitor curtailments, we believe forward EBITDA will be higher than the $368.65 million run rate of the past 6 years.

Catalyst: 4Q24 earnings report

The market will likely need to see results before the stock recovers toward fair value, but that could happen as early as the 4Q24 earnings report on January 27th.

Heading into 4Q, lumber prices were still at extremely low levels, so PCH likely guided accordingly. Guidance probably does not include the sharp increase in lumber prices in November, which sets PCH up for significantly higher than expected EBITDA in November and December.

A big beat on 4Q earnings could start the move toward fair value, which we see as about 30% above current market pricing.

data.title: Saul Centers: Access To Affluent Shoppers Will Fuel Growth
data.description:
Saul Centers (NYSE:BFS) is an old-line shopping center REIT with its properties concentrated in the Washington DC MSA. Despite its tenure, the company never really came across our radar because it consistently traded at a premium FFO multiple. Back in 2015 it traded at 20X while other shopping center REITs were far cheaper.

Saul’s multiple dropped to about 11X during the pandemic but even that wasn’t cheap because REITs were so absurdly discounted in 2020 that we were buying other shopping centers at 8X or lower.

Today, however, Saul trades at just over 12X FFO and 17X AFFO making it cheaper than the grocery-anchored shopping center averages of 14.6X and 17.8X FFO and AFFO respectively.

It is now interesting as a potential way to buy prosperous Washington DC properties at a significant discount to replacement cost. We bought a tiny starter position in BFS and began our due diligence.

We shall begin with a company overview of BFS and then follow with our analysis on fundamental outlook, valuation and the multitude of idiosyncratic factors to consider with this somewhat atypical company.

Saul Centers Overview

Saul Centers is a mid-cap REIT with properties consisting primarily of shopping centers with about a quarter of its portfolio in mixed-use apartment/office.

It has a few properties located along most of the East Coast, but the bulk of its assets are in DC.

We see this as a great market for shopping centers because DC has higher median household income than any other state, coming in at $106,049.

In combination with fairly high population density, Sauls’ shopping centers have access to a high number of affluent customers within their catchment radii.

Since the company has been around for a long time, long-term charts of key metrics serve as a means of understanding its track record.

FFO/share has grown moderately over time.

For most real estate sectors the above growth would be quite slow, but recall that the period from 2007 through 2018 was the dark ages for retail real estate. Shopping centers were overbuilt heading into the Financial Crisis and then the already oversupplied sector had to battle the advent of E-commerce.

Thus, while the growth rate was not impressive from a broader standpoint, it is more than adequate given the environment in which it was operating. As we have discussed more thoroughly here, fundamentals of the shopping center sector have improved dramatically. Shopping centers are now undersupplied and strong net absorption is allowing landlords to raise rents materially.

Thus, the forward growth rate for shopping centers should be much higher than it was historically. This is showing up in the numbers with Saul achieving greater than 6% same-store NOI growth in the most recent quarter.

Its earnings were fairly typical of retail REITs in 2024 with rental rates getting rolled up upon renewal/re-leasing.

Saul is among the more dividend focused shopping center REITs with a current yield of about 6%. Aside from a small cut from the Financial Crisis, Saul’s dividend has been stable to growing.

Given the fundamental outlook, we see its forward dividend as quite reliable, making it a reasonable income investment.

Growth Potential

Occupancy is roughly full with the typical frictional level of vacancy, so most of Saul’s growth potential comes from rental rate growth.

Saul’s rent per square foot is about $24 for its retail portfolio, which strikes me as well below market rates, particularly for the DC area. Saul’s leasing strategy is less aggressive than most with a clear preference to renew existing tenants rather than finding new tenants. Doing so allows them to forgo most of the tenant improvement costs and other capex but it likely leaves money on the table with regard to rental rates. 84.7% renewal rate is very high and I would argue a bit too high.

That said, the currently well below market rents can be unlocked in the future as rents roll.

I would anticipate forward organic growth around 5% annually with some fluctuation based on the percentage of existing leases expiring each year. More expiration would translate to more growth given the magnitude of mark-to-market.

Valuation

Saul is quite discounted relative to its own history, but a purchase decision today is not based on it being a better buy than it was. The hurdle it must overcome is being better than shopping center peers. For this, we can turn to a relative valuation.

As discussed above, Saul trades at a lower multiple than peers, but it also higher leverage than most of the sector.

Below we plotted the AFFO multiple of each shopping center REIT on the Y axis against the debt to capital on the X axis.

Those with higher leverage should trade at lower multiples and indeed they do as seen with the trendline.

On a leverage neutral basis, Saul trades right in-line with peers.

Absolute valuation is also worth considering here and it is perhaps the area where Saul looks more opportunistic. Consensus NAV is $52.50, so its $39.70 price tag represents a substantial discount.

Analysts forming the consensus are using a 6.61% cap rate in valuing Saul’s properties and that feels about right given age, location and type.

DC shopping centers would typically go for higher prices (maybe a 5.5% cap rate), but Saul’s properties are a bit higher average age which would pull the cap rate back up.

Idiosyncratic oddities

BFS is a quiet company. It doesn’t raise capital very often, so it operates more like a private company than a public. It doesn’t do quarterly conference calls and rarely puts out investor presentations. We have seen similar levels of quietness among One Liberty Properties (OLP) and Urstadt Biddle (UBA) prior to UBA being bought out.

Another strange detail is that BFS has extremely high insider ownership. 35.5% of common shares are held by B.F. Saul Real Estate Investment Trust. An additional 3.14% of common shares are held by B. F. Saul Co.

Additionally, there are 10 million OP units, many of which are owned by the Saul Family.

Per the 10-Q:

“As of September 30, 2024, the B. F. Saul Company and certain other affiliated entities, each of which is controlled by B. Francis Saul II and his family members, (collectively, the “Saul Organization”) held an aggregate 29.0% limited partnership interest in the Operating Partnership represented by approximately 10.0 million convertible limited partnership units. These units are convertible into shares of Saul Centers’ common stock, at the option of the unit holder, on a one-for-one basis provided that, in accordance with the Company’s Articles of Incorporation, the rights may not be exercised at any time that the Saul Organization beneficially owns or will own after the exercise, directly or indirectly, in the aggregate more than 39.9% of the value of the outstanding common stock and preferred stock of Saul Centers, excluding shares credited to directors’ deferred fee accounts (See Note 8). As of September 30, 2024, approximately 628,000 units could be converted into shares of Saul Centers common stock.”

Insider ownership of this magnitude is a double-edged sword. On the positive side, management is financially aligned with shareholders and on the negative side, the Saul family has nearly impenetrable control of the company.

It is difficult to say if this is overall positive or negative. Thus far, I have not been able to detect any signs of management abusing their entrenched position. It is merely something to make note of.

Buyout potential

Among the shopping center REITs a few stand out as the most ripe for getting bought out.

  • Whitestone (WSR) has repeatedly been the subject of hostile takeover attempts
  • Slate Grocery (OTC:SRRTF) and Saul each trade at steep discounts to NAV

Due to its small size, Slate is a highly accretive acquisition target because there would be substantial opex savings.

Saul stands out as a potential target due to its property concentration in DC. It is a highly desirable submarket and it would be difficult to otherwise assemble such a portfolio so the prospect of getting that exposure at a discount to NAV could be enticing to peer REITs or private equity.

Overall take

Saul’s valuation looks about right relative to its peers although I think the entire shopping center sector is opportunistic given the fundamental outlook being stronger than multiples imply. It has a strong property portfolio but some internal oddities which along with high leverage make it slightly riskier than peers.

Overall, it is not a slam dunk but potentially a good investment. We will watch it and perhaps buy more if it gets cheaper or there are signs of more imminent M&A.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.
